audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 13, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Amnon's obsession, Jonadab's grooming plan, David's facilitating command, Tamar's labor and resistance, rape, expulsion, servant participation, survivor lament, Absalom's silencing, David's inaction, revenge killing, false report, flight, and royal grief.

verses.restored: After this, Absalom son of David had a beautiful sister named Tamar, and Amnon son of David claimed love for her.
Amnon was distressed until he made himself ill because of Tamar his sister. She was a virgin, and it seemed difficult in eyes of Amnon to do anything to her.
Amnon had a companion named Jonadab son of Shimeah, David's brother. Jonadab was a very cunning man.
He said to him, Why are you, son of king, becoming thin morning by morning? Will you not tell me? Amnon said, I love Tamar, sister of Absalom my brother.

Jonadab said, Lie upon your bed and make yourself sick. When your father comes to see you, say, Please let Tamar my sister come and give me bread, preparing food before my eyes so I may see and eat from her hand.
Amnon lay down and made himself sick. King came to see him. Amnon said to king, Please let Tamar my sister come and make two heart-cakes before my eyes, that I may eat from her hand.
David sent to Tamar at house, saying, Please go to house of Amnon your brother and prepare food for him.
Tamar went to house of Amnon her brother while he lay down. She took dough, kneaded it, shaped cakes before his eyes, and cooked them.
She took pan and poured them before him, but he refused to eat. Amnon said, Send every man out from me. Every man went out from him.
Amnon said to Tamar, Bring food into inner room, that I may eat from your hand. Tamar took cakes she had made and brought them to Amnon her brother in inner room.
She brought them near to him to eat. He seized her and said to her, Come, lie with me, my sister.
She said to him, No, my brother. Do not violate me, because such thing is not done in Israel. Do not do this disgraceful folly.
Where could I carry my shame? You would be like one of fools in Israel. Now please speak to king, because he will not withhold me from you.
He was not willing to listen to her voice. He was stronger than she, violated her, and lay with her.
Then Amnon hated her with a very great hatred, for hatred with which he hated her was greater than love with which he had claimed to love her. Amnon said to her, Rise, go.
She said to him, No—sending me away is a greater evil than the other you did to me. But he was not willing to listen to her.
He called his young servant who attended him and said, Please send this one out from me, outside, and bolt door after her.
She wore a long ornamented robe, for thus daughters of king who were virgins dressed. His servant brought her outside and bolted door after her.
Tamar placed ashes upon her head, tore long ornamented robe that was upon her, placed her hand upon her head, and walked, crying aloud as she went.

Absalom her brother said to her, Has Amnon your brother been with you? Now, my sister, be silent; he is your brother. Do not set your heart on this thing. Tamar remained devastated/desolate in house of Absalom her brother.
King David heard all these things and became very angry.
Absalom did not speak with Amnon either good or evil, because Absalom hated Amnon for violating Tamar his sister.
After two full years, Absalom had sheep-shearers at Baal-hazor near Ephraim, and Absalom invited all sons of king.
Absalom came to king and said, Look, your servant has sheep-shearers. Please let king and his servants go with your servant.
King said to Absalom, No, my son, let us not all go, lest we be burdensome to you. He pressed him, but king was not willing to go and blessed him.
Absalom said, If not, please let Amnon my brother go with us. King said to him, Why should he go with you?
Absalom pressed him, and he sent Amnon and all sons of king with him.
Absalom commanded his servants, saying, Watch when heart of Amnon is good with wine. When I say to you, Strike Amnon, kill him. Do not fear. Have I not commanded you? Be strong and become courageous.
Servants of Absalom did to Amnon as Absalom commanded. All sons of king arose, each mounted his mule, and fled.
While they were on way, report came to David, saying, Absalom has struck all sons of king, and not one of them remains.
King arose, tore his garments, and lay upon ground. All his servants stood with garments torn.

Jonadab son of Shimeah, brother of David, answered and said, Do not let my lord say they killed all young men, sons of king. Amnon alone is dead, because by mouth/intention of Absalom this was set from day he violated Tamar his sister.
Now do not let my lord king set word on his heart, saying all sons of king died, because Amnon alone is dead.
Absalom fled. Young watchman lifted his eyes and looked, and there was much people coming from road behind him, from side of mountain.
Jonadab said to king, Look, sons of king have come. According to word of your servant, so it happened.
As he finished speaking, sons of king came, lifted their voice, and wept. King and all his servants also wept with very great weeping.
Absalom fled and went to Talmai son of Ammihud, king of Geshur. David mourned over his son all days.
Absalom fled and went to Geshur, and was there three years.
King David ceased/longed to go out toward Absalom, because he was comforted concerning Amnon, that he was dead.

translation_notes: Amnon's fixation is not love. Desire that plots isolation, ignores refusal, uses force, and turns to hatred is entitlement and sexual violence.
Jonadab identifies a vulnerable access route and designs grooming through illness, family trust, caregiving labor, and royal command.
David orders Tamar into Amnon's house. The chapter gives no indication that he evaluates her safety or later provides justice.
Tamar says no repeatedly, names the act as violation and folly, reasons with Amnon, and proposes alternatives. Nothing she says or does constitutes consent.
Verse 14 must be translated plainly as rape/violation. Strength difference and refusal are explicit.
Amnon's hatred and expulsion are perpetrator behavior, not evidence that Tamar lost value or became shameful.
The servant who removes and locks out Tamar participates in institutional abandonment after violence.
Tamar's ashes, torn robe, hand on head, and public crying are survivor testimony. She is not responsible for family reputation.
Absalom gives her housing but also tells her to be silent and minimizes the matter. Shelter does not make silencing acceptable.
David becomes angry but takes no recorded protective or judicial action. Parental anger without accountability leaves the survivor unprotected.
Absalom's killing of Amnon is retaliatory execution after institutional failure, not survivor justice and not Tamar's responsibility.
The narrative follows male grief and political exile after verse 20; readers must resist allowing Tamar to disappear.
safety_note: Maximum anti-rape safeguards: Believe Tamar's explicit refusal. Do not call Amnon lovesick, blame Tamar's beauty, treat incest as the main harm rather than rape, demand survivor silence, praise revenge killing, or excuse David's inaction. Center survivor autonomy, trauma-informed care, public accountability, and protection from the perpetrator.
